sourceText: This means that an individual's moral growth and development originate from within oneself, rather than external factors.
The people are the most important element in a nation, followed by the spirits of the land and grain; the sovereign counts the least.
This highlights the principle of 'people as the root' in governance, emphasizing that the populace is the fundamental basis and holds precedence over the state's existence.

The feeling of commiseration is the beginning of benevolence; the feeling of shame and dislike is the beginning of righteousness; the feeling of deference and compliance is the beginning of propriety; the feeling of right and wrong is the beginning of wisdom.
This signifies that the feeling of pity for another's suffering is the beginning of 'benevolence' (仁), a kind and loving heart, and it stems from humanity's inherent goodness.

He was subjected to the severe punishment of castration (taizhi) for opposing an imperial decision. This brought not only extreme physical pain but also immense social and mental humiliation. His family and lineage also suffered greatly.

He survived with the sole determination to complete the 'Shiji', overcoming personal disgrace and suffering to pour all his passion into documenting historical truth. His brush became proof of his redemption from humiliation.
A man dies, but his name lives on.
This means that although the physical body perishes, one's achievements and reputation can be remembered forever.
When discussing matters of the world, one must first examine their root.
This emphasizes the need to deeply explore the origin of any issue to understand its true nature.
Learning and mastering the past is more important than knowing the new.
